title: Arithmetic Mean
content: The arithmetic mean of n observations x1, x2, ..., xn is their sum divided by n. For grouped data with class marks xi and frequencies fi, the mean equals the sum of fi*xi divided by the sum of fi. The assumed mean method and step-deviation method are useful shortcuts when data values are large.

title: Median for Ungrouped and Grouped Data
content: The median is the middle value when observations are arranged in ascending or descending order. For n observations: if n is odd, median = ((n+1)/2)th value; if n is even, median = average of (n/2)th and (n/2 + 1)th values. For grouped data, the median class is identified using cumulative frequency, then the exact median is interpolated using the median formula.
example: For the data 3, 5, 7, 9, 11 (n=5, odd), median = 3rd value = 7. For 3, 5, 7, 9 (n=4, even), median = (5+7)/2 = 6.

title: Mode for Ungrouped and Grouped Data
content: The mode is the value that occurs most frequently in a dataset. A dataset can be unimodal, bimodal, or multimodal. For grouped data, the modal class is the class with the highest frequency, and the exact mode is calculated using frequencies of the modal class and its neighbours. The empirical relation 3 Median = Mode + 2 Mean holds approximately for moderately skewed distributions.
example: For the data 2, 3, 3, 5, 7, 7, 7, 9, the mode is 7 since it appears 3 times, more than any other value.

title: Range and Mean Deviation
content: Range is the simplest measure of dispersion: Range = Maximum value - Minimum value. Mean deviation about a central value a (mean or median) is the average of absolute deviations from a. Mean deviation about the median is always less than or equal to mean deviation about any other value. For grouped data, multiply each absolute deviation by its frequency before averaging.

title: Variance and Standard Deviation
content: Variance is the mean of the squares of deviations from the arithmetic mean. Standard deviation is the positive square root of variance. A shortcut formula avoids computing the mean first: variance = (mean of squares) - (square of mean). For grouped data, replace sums with frequency-weighted sums. Standard deviation has the same unit as the data, making it more interpretable than variance.

title: Coefficient of Variation
content: The coefficient of variation (CV) is a dimensionless measure that expresses standard deviation as a percentage of the mean. It is used to compare the variability of two datasets that have different units or different means. A higher CV indicates greater relative variability. The dataset with a smaller CV is considered more consistent or stable.
example: Dataset A: mean = 50, SD = 5, CV = (5/50)*100 = 10%. Dataset B: mean = 100, SD = 8, CV = (8/100)*100 = 8%. Dataset B is more consistent since its CV is lower.

title: Classical Definition of Probability
content: If a random experiment has n equally likely, mutually exclusive, and exhaustive outcomes, and m of these are favourable to event A, then P(A) = m/n. Probability always lies between 0 and 1, inclusive. P(sure event) = 1 and P(impossible event) = 0. The complement of event A is A', and P(A') = 1 - P(A).
formula_latex: P(A) = \frac{\text{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\text{Total number of outcomes}} = \frac{m}{n}, \quad 0 \le P(A) \le 1
example: When rolling a fair die, P(getting a number greater than 4) = favourable outcomes {5, 6} / total outcomes {1,2,3,4,5,6} = 2/6 = 1/3.

title: Addition Theorem of Probability
content: For any two events A and B, the probability that at least one occurs is given by the addition theorem. For mutually exclusive events (A and B cannot occur simultaneously), P(A intersection B) = 0, so the formula simplifies to P(A union B) = P(A) + P(B). This extends to three events with inclusion-exclusion.
formula_latex: P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)
example: A card is drawn from a standard deck. P(King) = 4/52, P(Heart) = 13/52, P(King and Heart) = 1/52. P(King or Heart) = 4/52 + 13/52 - 1/52 = 16/52 = 4/13.

title: Conditional Probability
content: The conditional probability of event A given that event B has already occurred is denoted P(A|B). It restricts the sample space to outcomes in B. Two events are independent if P(A|B) = P(A), equivalently P(A intersection B) = P(A)*P(B). The multiplication rule P(A intersection B) = P(B)*P(A|B) follows directly from the definition.
example: Two cards are drawn without replacement. P(2nd is Ace | 1st is Ace) = 3/51, since after removing one ace, 3 aces remain among 51 cards.

title: Theorem of Total Probability
content: If B1, B2, ..., Bn form a partition of the sample space (mutually exclusive and exhaustive events), then the probability of any event A can be expressed as a weighted sum using the law of total probability. This is fundamental to Bayes' theorem and is used when the probability of A depends on which partition event has occurred.
example: A factory has 3 machines producing 30%, 45%, 25% of output with defect rates 2%, 3%, 1%. P(defective) = 0.30*0.02 + 0.45*0.03 + 0.25*0.01 = 0.006 + 0.0135 + 0.0025 = 0.022 or 2.2%.

title: Bayes' Theorem
content: Bayes' theorem provides a way to update the probability of a hypothesis given new evidence. Given a partition B1, B2, ..., Bn of the sample space, the posterior probability P(Bi|A) is computed using the prior probabilities P(Bi) and likelihoods P(A|Bi). It reverses the conditioning: from P(A|Bi) to P(Bi|A). Widely used in medical testing, spam filtering, and decision-making.
example: Using the factory example: P(Machine 2 | defective) = (0.45*0.03)/0.022 = 0.0135/0.022 = 0.6136 or about 61.4%.

title: Applications of Bayes' Theorem
content: Bayes' theorem is commonly applied to medical diagnosis problems (sensitivity and specificity of tests), quality control in manufacturing, and decision problems under uncertainty. The key steps are: (1) identify the partition events (hypotheses), (2) list priors P(Bi), (3) list likelihoods P(A|Bi), (4) compute total probability P(A), (5) apply Bayes' formula for the desired posterior.
example: A disease affects 1% of a population. A test has 99% sensitivity and 95% specificity. P(Disease|Positive) = (0.99*0.01)/((0.99*0.01)+(0.05*0.99)) = 0.0099/0.0594 = 0.1667 or about 16.7%.

title: Discrete Random Variables and Probability Distribution
content: A random variable X is a real-valued function defined on a sample space. A discrete random variable takes countable values x1, x2, ..., xn with respective probabilities p1, p2, ..., pn. The probability distribution (or PMF) satisfies: pi >= 0 for all i, and the sum of all pi equals 1. The cumulative distribution function F(x) = P(X <= x) is a non-decreasing step function.
example: For a fair coin tossed twice, let X = number of heads. P(X=0) = 1/4, P(X=1) = 2/4 = 1/2, P(X=2) = 1/4. Verify: 1/4 + 1/2 + 1/4 = 1.

title: Continuous Random Variables
content: A continuous random variable can take any value in an interval. Its probability distribution is described by a probability density function (PDF) f(x) where f(x) >= 0 and the total area under the curve equals 1. The probability that X lies in [a, b] is the integral of f(x) from a to b. For a continuous RV, P(X = c) = 0 for any specific value c.
formula_latex: P(a \le X \le b) = \int_a^b f(x)\, dx, \quad \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\, dx = 1
example: If f(x) = 2x for 0 <= x <= 1 and 0 otherwise, then P(0.5 <= X <= 1) = integral from 0.5 to 1 of 2x dx = [x^2] from 0.5 to 1 = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75.

title: Expectation (Mean) of a Random Variable
content: The mathematical expectation or expected value E(X) is the long-run average value of a random variable. For discrete RVs, it is the sum of each value times its probability. Key properties: E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b (linearity), E(X + Y) = E(X) + E(Y) for any X, Y. Expected value need not be a value that X can actually take.
formula_latex: E(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot P(X = x_i), \quad E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b

title: Variance of a Random Variable
content: The variance Var(X) measures how spread out the values of X are around the mean. It equals the expected value of the squared deviation from the mean. The shortcut formula Var(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 is computationally convenient. Key properties: Var(aX + b) = a^2 Var(X), Var(X) >= 0, and Var(constant) = 0.

title: Fundamental Principle of Counting and nPr
content: The multiplication principle states: if one task can be done in m ways and a second task in n ways, both together can be done in m*n ways. A permutation is an arrangement of objects where order matters. The number of permutations of r objects chosen from n distinct objects is nPr = n!/(n-r)!. Special case: n! is the number of permutations of all n objects.
example: Number of 3-letter arrangements from {A, B, C, D, E} = 5P3 = 5!/(5-3)! = 120/2 = 60.

title: Permutations with Repetition and Identical Objects
content: When r objects are chosen from n types with unlimited repetition, the number of arrangements is n^r. When permuting n objects where p1 are of type 1, p2 of type 2, ..., pk of type k, the number of distinct permutations is n!/(p1! * p2! * ... * pk!). This handles problems like arranging letters in a word with repeated letters.
example: Arrangements of the letters in MISSISSIPPI: 11 letters with M=1, I=4, S=4, P=2. Answer = 11!/(1!*4!*4!*2!) = 39916800/1152 = 34650.

title: Circular Permutations
content: In a circular arrangement, one position is fixed to avoid counting rotations of the same arrangement. The number of ways to arrange n distinct objects in a circle is (n-1)!. If clockwise and anticlockwise arrangements are considered identical (as in a necklace or bracelet), the count is (n-1)!/2.
example: 8 people sit around a circular table: arrangements = (8-1)! = 7! = 5040. If the table has no distinguishable orientation (like a rotating platform), it remains 5040 since people are distinguishable. For 8 beads on a bracelet: (8-1)!/2 = 5040/2 = 2520.

title: Combinations (nCr) and Properties
content: A combination is a selection of objects where order does not matter. The number of ways to choose r objects from n distinct objects is nCr = n!/(r!(n-r)!). Key properties: nC0 = nCn = 1, nCr = nC(n-r) (symmetry), nCr + nC(r-1) = (n+1)Cr (Pascal's identity). Combinations are the building blocks of the binomial theorem.
example: Choosing a committee of 3 from 7 people = 7C3 = 7!/(3!*4!) = 5040/(6*24) = 35.

title: Applications of Combinations
content: Combinations are used in problems involving selection of teams, distributing objects, choosing subsets, and counting paths in grids. Common problem types: selecting r items from n with constraints (at least, at most, exactly), distributing identical objects into distinct groups (stars and bars), and counting diagonals or triangles from polygon vertices.
formula_latex: \text{Diagonals of n-gon} = \binom{n}{2} - n = \frac{n(n-3)}{2}, \quad \text{Triangles from n points} = \binom{n}{3}
example: Number of diagonals of an octagon (n=8) = 8C2 - 8 = 28 - 8 = 20. Number of triangles from 10 non-collinear points = 10C3 = 120.

title: Bernoulli Trials and Binomial Distribution
content: A Bernoulli trial is an experiment with exactly two outcomes: success (probability p) and failure (probability q = 1 - p). The binomial distribution models the number of successes X in n independent Bernoulli trials. X can take values 0, 1, 2, ..., n. It is denoted X ~ B(n, p). Conditions: fixed number of trials, independent trials, constant probability of success, and two outcomes per trial.
formula_latex: P(X = r) = \binom{n}{r} p^r q^{n-r}, \quad r = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n, \quad q = 1 - p
example: A fair coin is tossed 5 times. P(exactly 3 heads) = 5C3 * (1/2)^3 * (1/2)^2 = 10 * 1/8 * 1/4 = 10/32 = 5/16.

title: Mean and Variance of Binomial Distribution
content: For X ~ B(n, p), the mean (expected value) is np and the variance is npq where q = 1 - p. The standard deviation is sqrt(npq). The mean is always greater than or equal to the variance since q <= 1. The distribution is symmetric when p = 0.5, right-skewed when p < 0.5, and left-skewed when p > 0.5.
example: A biased coin with P(head) = 0.6 is tossed 20 times. Mean = 20*0.6 = 12 heads. Variance = 20*0.6*0.4 = 4.8. SD = sqrt(4.8) = 2.19.

title: Binomial Distribution - Advanced Problems
content: Advanced binomial problems involve finding the most likely value (mode), computing cumulative probabilities P(X >= k) or P(X <= k), and using the recurrence relation P(X=r+1)/P(X=r) = (n-r)p/((r+1)q) to build the distribution efficiently. The mode of B(n,p) lies at floor((n+1)p) or (n+1)p - 1 when (n+1)p is an integer. JEE problems often involve finding n and p given mean and variance.
example: If mean = 6 and variance = 4 for a binomial RV, then np = 6 and npq = 4, so q = 4/6 = 2/3, p = 1/3, n = 18. Mode = floor(19 * 1/3) = floor(6.33) = 6.

title: Combined Mean and Variance of Two Groups
content: When two groups of sizes n1 and n2 with means x1_bar, x2_bar and variances s1^2, s2^2 are combined, the combined mean and variance can be computed without going back to individual observations. This is useful in problems where statistics of subgroups are known and the overall statistics are required. The combined variance accounts for both within-group and between-group variability.
